title: Spinning Teeswater wool
subtitle: Working the long, lustrous locks of this northern English longwool breed
excerpt: Teeswater is a Northern English longwool breed with a silky, wavy fleece and a staple length of 20 to 40 cm. This entry covers preparing the locks, choosing the right draft method, and spinning a smooth, lustrous two-ply yarn.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from British longwool spinning practice. Teeswater is a heritage breed native to the Tees Valley; its long, silky locks are described in the Rare Breeds Survival Trust breed profiles and in Lexi Boeger's Intertwined (2009, in copyright, substance only referenced). Technical fibre properties from open breed databases.

glossaryTerms.definition: The length of an individual fleece lock from the cut end to the tip, measured after washing. Teeswater staple typically runs 20 to 40 cm, placing it among the longest longwool breeds.
A spinning method in which fibres are drawn from a combed or combed-lock preparation with the hand held close to the orifice, producing a smooth, dense, low-loft yarn.
A yarn made by twisting two singles together in the opposite direction to the singles twist, producing a balanced, round yarn. The most common commercial and handspun yarn construction.

The reflective sheen present in longwool fleeces such as Teeswater, Wensleydale, and Lincoln. Lustre comes from the smooth, wide cuticle scales that cover the fibre surface, which reflect light like a flat mirror rather than scattering it.

content.text: Teeswater is one of the longest and most lustrous of the British heritage breeds. The individual locks hang in loose, wavy curls with a silky feel; the
is close to that of Wensleydale, though the fibre tends to be slightly coarser. The long
means the fibre needs little preparation beyond a flick with the flicker brush, and the
is the right choice for maintaining the sheen in the finished yarn.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing away from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Washed Teeswater fleece locks or commercially prepared Teeswater top

content.text: If working with raw locks, hold each lock by the cut end and give the tip a few passes with the flicker brush to open the tips without disturbing the lock structure. Avoid hand-carding Teeswater; carding breaks the long staple and destroys the lustre. If working from commercially combed top, simply pre-draft gently by pulling the top to about twice its original diameter before beginning to spin.

Set the wheel to a medium-to-fast flyer ratio (around 10:1) with light uptake tension. Spin using the worsted draw with a short backward draft; hold the fibre supply in the back hand and allow very little twist to enter the drafting zone. Teeswater spins smoothly and quickly but needs a little more twist than shorter wools to hold together. Aim for a consistent DK weight singles. Wind onto a bobbin and fill at least two bobbins before plying.

Ply two bobbins together by feeding them from the lazy kate onto the wheel in the opposite direction to the singles twist (S-twist singles are plied with Z-twist). Use a slow treadle speed and a low twist count; Teeswater does not need heavy plying to balance. The finished
should be smooth, round, and lustrous. Wind off onto the niddy-noddy, tie the hank in four places, and set the twist in lukewarm water for 20 minutes. Hang to dry under a light weight.

items.problem: The yarn has a dull surface despite the lustrous fibre
items.cause: The singles were over-twisted or the fibre was prepared by hand-carding
items.fix: Use less treadle speed relative to drafting speed. Prepare only with the flicker brush or by gently pre-drafting combed top. Carding breaks the fibre and creates a woollen-style yarn that does not retain lustre.

items.problem: The singles break repeatedly during spinning
items.cause: Too little twist, or the draft zone is too long for the staple length
items.fix: Teeswater's long staple means the drafting hand can move further back from the orifice, but the twist must still penetrate the full draft zone. Increase treadle speed slightly relative to backward draft speed, or shorten the draft zone.
